name: Comparison of Classes by Name
description: The product compares classes by name, which can cause it to use the wrong class when multiple classes can have the same name.
extended_description: If the decision to trust the methods and data of an object is based on the name of a class, it is possible for malicious users to send objects of the same name as trusted classes and thereby gain the trust afforded to known classes and types.

mapping.rationale: This CWE entry is at the Variant level of abstraction, which is a preferred level of abstraction for mapping to the root causes of vulnerabilities.

mechanism.root_cause_summary: The product compares classes by name, which can cause it to use the wrong class when multiple classes can have the same name.
typical_preconditions: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
typical_sources: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
negative_nonfix_signals: style refactor without changing the sensitive path

guard_patterns: same logical object and same sink context

recommended_guard_templates: same logical object and same sink context

stage1.goal: Classify patch chunks by how directly they repair the CWE root cause.
must_focus: direct change to vulnerable sink or validation site
changes that alter boundary, lifecycle, policy, or authorization relation
supporting helper or error-propagation logic needed by the main fix
must_avoid: generic logging changes without security semantics
refactors unrelated to the sensitive sink/path

stage2.goal: Construct discriminative root cause, anchor, predicates, and guards.
must_focus: anchor at the sink or dominant validation block
prefer predicates that encode relations, not lone generic tokens
choose function-local or anchor-window scope whenever possible
must_avoid: predicates that rely on generic names alone
anchors in wrappers that lack the core vulnerability mechanism
recommended_queries: same logical object and same sink context

stage3.goal: Verify whether the target tag still exhibits the vulnerable relation or already contains the fix relation.
must_focus: whether the sensitive sink/path still exists
whether the fix relation is locally present in the same code region
whether anchor relocation is required before concluding absence
must_avoid: declaring NOT_AFFECTED from generic token absence alone
using broad token matches as final evidence

bad_examples.summary: In this example, the expression in the if statement compares the class of the inputClass object to a trusted class by comparing the class names.
bad_examples.snippet: if (inputClass.getClass().getName().equals("TrustedClassName")) { // Do something assuming you trust inputClass // ... }

bad_examples.summary: In this example, the Java class, TrustedClass, overrides the equals method of the parent class Object to determine equivalence of objects of the class. The...
bad_examples.snippet: public class TrustedClass { ... @Override public boolean equals(Object obj) { boolean isEquals = false; // first check to see if the object is of the same class if...

good_examples.summary: In this example, the expression in the if statement compares the class of the inputClass object to a trusted class by comparing the class names.
good_examples.snippet: if (inputClass.getClass() == TrustedClass.class) { // Do something assuming you trust inputClass // ... }

good_examples.summary: In this example, the Java class, TrustedClass, overrides the equals method of the parent class Object to determine equivalence of objects of the class. The...
good_examples.snippet: public boolean equals(Object obj) { ... // first check to see if the object is of the same class if (obj.getClass() == this.getClass()) { ... } ... }
